e39e8af0e0f5085f.subject: Physics
e39e8af0e0f5085f.question:
In a Young's Double Slit Experiment (YDSE), the slits are separated by 0.45 mm and the screen is placed 2.4 m away.
The distance between the central bright fringe and the fourth bright fringe is measured to be 1.2 cm.
If the entire experimental setup is immersed in a transparent liquid of refractive index μ=1.7, what will be the new fringe width?
e39e8af0e0f5085f.answer:
Ans - 0.176...
D = 2.4 m  - this info not required
d = 0.45 mm  - this info is not even required
4*beta = 1.2 cm
refractive index = 1.7
new beta = ((1.2/4)))/1.7 = 0.176... cm

8dbf5df36a67d83c.subject: Physics
8dbf5df36a67d83c.question:
Light of frequency 7.21×10^14 Hz is incident on a metal surface.
Electrons are ejected with a maximum speed of 6.0×10^5 m/s.
Calculate the work function of the metal in electron volts (eV).
(Given: h=6.63×10−34 Js, mass of an electron = 9.1×10−31 kg, 1 eV=1.6×10−19 J).
8dbf5df36a67d83c.answer:
Ans - 1.96 eV

KE = 1/2 mv^2
v = 6*10^5 m/s
total energy = h*freq
freq = 7.21*10^14 Hz
total energy - KE = work function
by plugging in the values, work function in J -
to convert to eV - divide by 1.6 *10^-19

039d3c43d172c7ce.subject: Physics
039d3c43d172c7ce.question: A parallel plate capacitor is connected to a battery, and then the battery is disconnected. A dielectric slab is then inserted. How does the electric field change?
039d3c43d172c7ce.answer:
The trick lies in the sequence: connected (gets charged)
->disconnected (charge is conserved) -> dielectric inserted.
Q remains constant, but V decreases (because
V=V/Kappa), so E decreases (because E=V/d). If the
battery was still connected, V would be constant and Q
would increase.

170711be4c460c78.subject: English
170711be4c460c78.question: Extrapolate on the pretext of the poem "Road Not Taken" by Robert Frost on the basis of the situation of the world at the time it was written and the condition of the poet at that time
170711be4c460c78.answer:
This poem was written during the time of the
WW1. The poet, at that time, was at a crucial
time of his life, where he was faced with two
choices that could decide his future. In his case,
he wrote this poem as an ode to a friend who had trouble
making simple decisions. The poem reflects
on their journey on making choices. In the first two stanzas
of the poem, he describes two choices as two paths
at a crossroad. He explains how he tries to look
down as far as possible in both paths referring to
his attempt to forsee his future. However he
explains how this foresight is barred to only a
specific point after which everything is a mystery.
He also describes both paths, one of which is
less trodden upon. This reflects in his life as writing
(his current proffesion) was not a very popular
choice taken up at that time. In the next two stanzas,
he describes his life after he has made his decision
to take the less popular path of writing. He seemed
to know that after he made his decision, it would l
ead onto other ways which would make it impossible
to come back to this one. In the end, he accepts
this and makes his way down his own path. He
ends the poem with a slightly ambiguous note by
saying "I shall be telling this with a sigh".This sigh
may symbolise many emotions such as contentment,
regret, curiousity,etc. This interpretation has been
left to the reader to figure out. To wrap up, the poet
says that the choice was a life - changing one that
made all the difference to him.

38545f95dc2daf6a.subject: Summarisation
38545f95dc2daf6a.question: Refer to the NCERT Class 12 Computer Science textbook and write comprehensive notes covering all the main points in the chapter: Computer Networks - Protocols
38545f95dc2daf6a.answer:
Network Protocol
Network protocols set the rules and standards that devices use to talk to each other over a network. Here are some important protocols: HTTP, FTP, PPP, SMTP, TCP/IP, POP3, HTTPS, TELNET, and VoIP.

1. HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)

Used to send web pages and the files that go along with them across the Internet.
Port: 80
When you go to a website, HTTP is the method used to ask for and get web pages from servers.
Example: Browsing a website like www.example.com.
HTTP-Protocol
HTTP Protocol
2. FTP (File Transfer Protocol)

The job is to move files from one computer to another across a network.
Port: 21 (default)
Usage: It is used for uploading or downloading files from a server.
Uploading the files of a website to a server where the website will be hosted.
File-Transfer-Protocol-and-HTTPS-gif-2
FTP Protocol
3. PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)

Function: Creates a direct link between two network devices, often used for dial-up connections.
Used to connect to the internet through a phone line or a serial connection.
Example: Dial-up Internet connections in the past.
PPP Protocol Suite
PPP Protocol Suite
4. SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol)

Function: Sends and routes email between servers.
Port: 25
Usage: Email servers use it to send messages to other servers or to individual users.
Sending an email from user@example.com to user2@example.com.
SMTP-Gif
SMTP Protocol
5. TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol)

Function: A group of rules that control how information is sent across the Internet.
Usage: Guarantees that data is sent reliably, in the right order, and without errors between devices.
The basic rules that are used for communication over the Internet.
TCP/IP-Gif
Working of TCP/IP
6. POP3 (Post Office Protocol version 3)
The job is to get emails from a server and send them to a client.
Port: 110
Usage: It is used to bring emails from a mail server to an email client.
Accessing your email through an email client such as Outlook.
7. HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure)
Function: A secure type of HTTP that protects data by encrypting it to keep information private and safe.
Port: 443
Used for sending messages safely across the Internet, like when you do online banking or shop online.
Example: Accessing a secure website like https://www.example.com/
HTTPS-gif
HTTPS Protocol
8. TELNET
The function offers a way to communicate with other systems from a command line remotely.
Port: 23
Usage: Lets users sign in and work with distant servers or machines.
Accessing a remote server to perform command-line tasks.

9. VoIP (Voice over Internet Protocol)
Function: Enables voice communication over the Internet.
Usage: It is used to make voice calls through internet-based networks rather than using regular phone lines.
Example: Services like Skype or Zoom can be used for making voice or video calls.

1fcfa7e800b493e9.subject: Summarisation
1fcfa7e800b493e9.question: Refer to the NCERT Class 12 Physics textbook and write comprehensive notes covering all the main topics in the chapter: Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance
1fcfa7e800b493e9.answer:
Electric potential and capacitance come from the idea of charge. The charge is found by looking at how many protons and electrons are in a material. A material that has more protons than electrons has a positive charge, and a material that has more electrons than protons has a negative charge.

If the numbers are the same, the material is neutral. Electric charges create forces that push or pull on each other.Charges that are opposite, like positive and negative, pull towards each other.Charges that are the same, like two positives or two negatives, push away from each other. Charge is measured in units called coulombs, written as C.Each proton has a positive charge of +1.602 × 10−19 C, and each electron has a negative charge of −1.602 × 10−19 C.

Electrostatic Potential
The electrostatic potential at a point in an electric field is the amount of work required to bring a single positive charge from a very far away place to that specific point without making it speed up or slow down.

The unit used to measure electric potential is joules per coulomb, which shows how much work is done for each unit of charge. The J/C unit is usually referred to as a volt (V).

Formula of electrostatic potential,
V = W/q

Where,

V = Electrostatic potential (Volts)
W = Work done (Joules)
q = Charge (Coulombs)

Electrostatic Potential Due to a Point Charge
The potential at a distance 'r' from a charge 'Q' is given by.

𝑽 = 𝒌𝑸/𝑟

Where,

k = Coulomb's constant (9
×
× 109 𝑵𝒎2/𝑪2)
Q = Charge (Coulombs)
r = Distance from charge (meters)

Capacitance
A capacitor is a tool that holds electric power. The capacitance of a capacitor is the amount of charge it can store compared to the voltage applied across it.

The unit used to measure capacitance is coulomb per volt (C/V), which shows how much charge is stored for each volt of electricity applied. The farad is the standard unit used to measure capacitance, and it is often used instead of the unit C/V.

Formula of Capacitor is,
𝑪 = 𝑸/𝑽

Where,

C = Capacitance (Farads, F)
Q = Charge stored (Coulombs)
V = Potential difference (Volts)

Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor
The capacitance of a capacitor that has two flat plates placed parallel to each other, with a distance 'd' between them and each having an area 'A', is calculated by using the formula.

U =
ε
ε 0 𝐀 / D

Where,

\varepsilon0 = Permittivity of free space (8.85
×
× 10-12 F/m
A = Plate area (square meters)
d = Separation distance (meters)

Energy Stored in a Capacitor
The energy stored in a charged capacitor is given by.

U = 1/2 CV2

Where,

U = Energy stored (Joules)
C = Capacitance (Farads)
V = Voltage (Volts)

32d78cde89e0f8e1.subject: Summarisation
32d78cde89e0f8e1.question: https://www.planetebook.com/free-ebooks/the-great-gatsby.pdf - go through this and write a summary on the book
32d78cde89e0f8e1.answer:
Nick Carraway, a young man from Minnesota, moves to New York during the summer of 1922 to learn about working in the bond business. He rents a house in the West Egg part of Long Island, which is a rich but not very stylish area.This place is full of people who became wealthy recently, so they don't have old friends or social ties.These people tend to show off their money in flashy ways. Nick's neighbor in West Egg is a mysterious man named Jay Gatsby.He lives in a huge, old-fashioned mansion and hosts big, fancy parties every Saturday night.

Nick is different from the other people in West Egg—he went to Yale and knows people in East Egg, which is a stylish part of Long Island where the wealthy, old-money families live. Nick goes to East Egg one evening to have dinner with his cousin Daisy Buchanan and her husband, Tom, who was once Nick's classmate at Yale. Daisy and Tom bring Nick meet Jordan Baker, a pretty and tough-minded young lady, and Nick starts a love relationship with her. Nick finds out a little about Daisy and Tom's marriage.Jordan tells him that Tom has a girlfriend named Myrtle Wilson.Myrtle lives in the valley of ashes, which is a dark, dirty area between West Egg and New York City. Soon after finding out this truth, Nick goes to New York City with Tom and Myrtle. At a messy, flashy party in the apartment Tom uses for the event, Myrtle starts to mock Tom about Daisy, and Tom reacts by breaking her nose.

As the summer goes on, Nick finally gets an invitation to one of Gatsby's famous parties. He meets Jordan Baker at the party and they meet Gatsby himself, a surprisingly young man who uses an English accent, has a great smile, and says “old sport” to everyone.Gatsby asks to talk to Jordan by himself, and through Jordan, Nick finds out more about his mysterious neighbor. Gatsby tells Jordan that he knew Daisy in Louisville back in 1917 and that he's really, really in love with her. He spends a lot of nights looking at the green light at the end of her dock, which is across the bay from his big house. Gatsby's fancy lifestyle and big parties are just his way of trying to win Daisy's attention. Gatsby now wants Nick to help him get together with Daisy again, but he's worried that Daisy might not want to see him if she finds out he still loves her. Nick asks Daisy to come to his home for tea, but he doesn't let her know that Gatsby will be there too. After a bit of an awkward get-together, Gatsby and Daisy start to feel connected again. Their love rekindled, they begin an affair.
After a while, Tom starts to feel more and more doubtful about his wife's connection with Gatsby. At a lunch at the Buchanans' home, Gatsby looks at Daisy with such clear and intense love that Tom understands Gatsby is in love with her. Even though Tom is having an affair with someone else, he is really upset if he thinks his wife might be cheating on him. He makes the group drive into New York City, and then he meets Gatsby in a room at the Plaza Hotel. Tom says that he and Daisy have a past that Gatsby can't possibly understand, and he tells his wife that Gatsby is a criminal—his money comes from selling illegal alcohol and other unlawful activities. Daisy realizes she really cares about Tom, and Tom treats her badly by sending her back to East Egg with Gatsby, trying to show that Gatsby can't do anything to hurt him.

When Nick, Jordan, and Tom drive through the valley of ashes, they find out that Gatsby's car hit and killed Myrtle, who was Tom's girlfriend. They hurry back to Long Island, where Nick finds out from Gatsby that Daisy was the one driving the car that hit Myrtle, but Gatsby plans to take the blame for it. The next day, Tom tells Myrtle's husband, George, that Gatsby was the one who was driving the car. George thinks the driver who killed Myrtle was her lover, so he goes to Gatsby's house, finds him in the pool, and kills him. He then fatally shoots himself.
Nick holds a small funeral for Gatsby, breaks off his relationship with Jordan, and returns to the Midwest to get away from the people around Gatsby's life, and from the hollowness and bad morals he sees in the wealthy lifestyle on the East Coast. Nick thinks that just like Gatsby's dream of Daisy turned into something bad because of money and lies, the American dream of happiness and being your own person has now become just about getting rich. Even though Gatsby's ability to turn his dreams into real things is what makes him 'great''Nick thinks that the time of dreaming—both Gatsby's dream and the American dream—is finished.'

c2e61f6d961221c0.subject: Summarisation
c2e61f6d961221c0.question: https://dn790007.ca.archive.org/0/items/cu31924013243963/cu31924013243963.pdf - refer to this and create a summary on this book
c2e61f6d961221c0.answer:
Matthew and Marilla Cuthbert are brothers and sisters who live together on their family's old farm called Green Gables in the small town of Avonlea, which is on Prince Edward Island in Canada. Matthew is sixty years old, and because he is getting too old to do all the farm work by himself, the Cuthberts decide to adopt an orphan boy to assist him. This decision shocks the town gossip, Mrs. Rachel Lynde believes that Matthew and Marilla are not fit to take care of a child.

Matthew is really scared of women.He goes to the train station and sees a girl instead of a boy.The orphanage sent an 11-year-old girl named Anne Shirley by mistake. Anne's lively talk and cheerful spirit charm Matthew, who shyly tells Marilla that he wants to keep her. Marilla starts off unsure, but after trying Anne out for a while, she decides to let her stay.

Anne is a cheerful and chatty girl even though she lives a poor life and doesn't have any family. Even though she doesn't have much social skills or formal education, she has a very rich and imaginative mind, and she's always positive and kind-hearted. Anne follows her feelings instead of following rules about how a lady should act, so she ends up not meeting the usual standards of proper behavior for a lady. She goes to church for the first time wearing a bunch of wildflowers in a wreath, and she shouts at Mrs. Rachel was making fun of her red hair. Anne puts a lot of effort into pleasing Marilla and following her social rules, but she often messes up.She uses liniment instead of vanilla in a cake, lets a mouse die in the plum-pudding sauce, and tries to pray before bed for the first time, saying a sincere but silly prayer.


Anne didn't have any real friends before she moved to Green Gables, so she made up imaginary friends to play with. In Avonlea, she meets Diana Barry, a neighbor who quickly becomes her close friend. One afternoon, Anne asks Diana to join her for tea and accidentally serves her red currant wine instead of the nonalcoholic raspberry cordial. Diana comes home drunk, and Diana’s mom thinks Anne made her drunk on purpose, so she tells the girls not to talk to each other anymore. The hard time of being apart lasts until Anne helps Diana's sister, who is very ill with croup, which makes Mrs. Barry to forgive her.
At school, Anne has a disagreement with a good-looking, clever boy named Gilbert Blythe. When they first meet, Gilbert teases Anne by calling her Carrots and pulling her red braid. Anne is very upset about her red hair, and Gilbert's jokes really bother her. She yells at him and hits him with a slate on the head. This event starts a competition between Anne and Gilbert, the two brightest students, that continues throughout the whole book.

As Anne gets older, she starts to lose some of her playful, dramatic, and romantic ways from when she was a child, and she begins to focus more on her studies with energy and enthusiasm. A kind teacher named Miss Stacy notices Anne's smartness and asks her to join a group of students who are getting ready for the entrance test at Queen’s Academy. Her long-time competition with Gilbert Blythe turns into a friendly and close rivalry when, after four years of not speaking to each other, they both end up at Queen’s Academy. Trying hard to make Matthew and Marilla proud, Anne focuses completely on her studies and manages to win the famous Avery Scholarship.This scholarship gives her enough money to go to college for four years in the fall.

Anne is excited about her future and heads back to Green Gables. Matthew, who has had issues with his heart, passes away due to a heart attack. When Anne finds out that Marilla might go blind, she chooses to stay at Green Gables and teach nearby so she can take care of Marilla, even though she had planned to get a four-year degree. Gilbert finds out about her choice and decides to quit his job as the teacher at Avonlea school so Anne can take his position and live closer to Marilla. After five years of competing against each other, Gilbert and Anne become good friends. Even though her options have become much fewer, Anne stays hopeful and looks forward to her future with a happy attitude.
